species: Chorus Frog + Owl + Cricket -- simultaneous multi-species night soundscape
scientific_names: Pseudacris regilla (Pacific Chorus Frog, Baird and Girard 1852)
sound_type: Simultaneous three-layer night soundscape: Layer 1 (low-mid, dense, continuous) Pacific Chorus Frog breeding chorus 500-3000 Hz; Layer 2 (low-mid, spaced) Great Horned Owl territorial 300-500 Hz; Layer 3 (high, continuous) Western Field Cricket stridulation 4000-8000 Hz. Frequency separation enables simultaneous audibility without masking. Three species occupying distinct registers, three spatial zones (water/woodland edge/ground level), three temporal patterns (dense-rhythmic/periodic/continuous).
ecosystem: Woodland pond, edge habitat, mixed conifer-deciduous forest adjacent. Puget Sound lowlands, Willamette Valley, western Cascades foothills. Vernal pools fish-free benefit for Pseudacris. Notable sites: Ridgefield NWR, Nisqually NWR, Woodard Bay NRCA.
season: Spring night (March-May). Temperature trigger: Pseudacris active above 50°F water temp; Bubo year-round but peak spring territorial; cricket above 55°F air temp at night. Full three-layer stack: April-May.
family: Multi-species -- Hylidae (tree frogs) + Strigidae (typical owls) + Gryllidae (crickets)
milestone: FIRST MULTI-SPECIES SIMULTANEOUS NIGHT SOUNDSCAPE in post-summit sequence. E=7 through superposition: no single layer reaches E=7 alone. Frequency coverage 300-8000 Hz. Three-layer vertical acoustic stack: ground level (crickets) + pond/edge (frogs) + elevated woodland (owl). First woodland pond ecosystem entry in post-summit SPS.

acoustic_data.layer_1_frog: Pseudacris regilla breeding chorus. 500-3000 Hz. 50-500 simultaneous males at productive pond. Complex interference pattern. The ribbit: ascending two-note call repeated at 0.5-1.5 second intervals. The dominant layer.
acoustic_data.layer_2_owl: Bubo virginianus territorial 5-note hoot. 300-500 Hz. One or two owls, 30-60 second call intervals. Woodland edge perch 10-30 feet up, 20-50m from pond. Frequency separation from frog chorus enables coexistence without masking.
acoustic_data.layer_3_cricket: Gryllus integer stridulation. 4000-8000 Hz. Continuous. Rate temperature-dependent: Dolbear's Law (1897). At 60°F: ~20 chirps/14 sec. Ground-level radiation, omnidirectional.
acoustic_data.e7_calibration: E=7 through superposition of three layers occupying distinct frequency registers (300-500 Hz / 500-3000 Hz / 4000-8000 Hz) and distinct spatial zones and distinct temporal patterns. Combined frequency coverage: 300-8000 Hz. No single layer E=7 alone.

nodes.title: Three-Layer Vertical Acoustic Stack
nodes.description: Ground level (cricket, high register) + pond/edge (frog, mid register) + elevated woodland perch (owl, low-mid register). Spatial vertical distribution + frequency register distribution = fully non-overlapping acoustic use of three-dimensional space.

nodes.title: Dolbear's Law -- Cricket as Thermometer
nodes.description: Amos Dolbear (1897): chirps per 14 seconds + 40 = temperature Fahrenheit. The cricket layer at degree 321 is simultaneously the high-frequency acoustic carrier and the spring night thermometer.

nodes.title: E=7 Through Superposition -- No Single Layer Sufficient
nodes.description: The degree-321 E=7 is reached through the simultaneous combination of three layers. This is a different calibration mechanism from single-source E=7 events (Great Horned Owl territorial, degree 299). E=7 as emergent property of the acoustic ecology.

cross_references.relationship: E=7 + E=7 = E=14. Multi-layer parallel: three musicians in three registers (Gas Huffer) vs. three species in three registers (spring pond). Simultaneous superposition as the shared E=7 mechanism.
Full territorial sequence at degree 299 (E=7 individual event). At degree 321, owl as one layer in larger soundscape. Individual E=7 vs. collective-layer contribution to superposition E=7.

Earlier multi-species simultaneous event. Dawn chorus: multiple avian species; spring night chorus: amphibian + avian + invertebrate. Both E=7 through superposition; different species assemblage and temporal context.
